title: CAP Act of 2025
plain_summary: The CAP Act of 2025 tells the Environmental Protection Agency to create a program that gives money to local governments and tribal groups. This money helps these communities make plans for dealing with climate change problems. The plans must look at how climate change could hurt people, animals, plants, and buildings in their area. The plans also need to explain what the community will do to fix or prevent these problems. Communities that have been hurt by pollution in the past will get first choice for this money.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Climate Adaptation Plan Act of two thousand twenty-five or the CAP Act of two thousand twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2 Climate Adaptation Plan Grant Program
sections.text: The head of the Environmental Protection Agency must create a program to give out money within one year after this law is passed. This money will help local governments and tribal groups make plans for dealing with climate change. The agency will choose who gets the money based on which applications are best. The agency head must work with different groups of people to create rules for how this program will work. These groups include young people, farmers, nonprofit organizations, scientists, labor unions, businesses, Native American communities, and local governments. To get money from this program, an applicant must fill out a form. The form must include proof that low-income communities live in their area. They must show they have experience with climate change studies, disaster planning, managing money for projects, and applying for federal grants. If they do not have this experience, they must plan to hire someone who does. The application must include information about the people they represent, such as how many people live there, how much money families make on average, what races live there, education levels, and what kinds of jobs people have. They must explain why they want to make a climate plan and describe how climate change is already hurting their community or might hurt it in the future. The application must explain how they will include different groups in making their plan, including young people, farmers, nonprofit groups, scientists, labor unions, businesses, Native American communities that will be affected, and nearby governments. They must say who will be in charge of making the plan and whether they will hire outside help. They must give a timeline for finishing the plan and explain how they will make sure environmental justice communities know about the planning process. When making their climate plan, applicants should try to connect it with other plans they already have. These might include disaster plans, land use plans, economic development plans, building improvement plans, community plans, and emergency plans. Every climate plan made with this grant money must include several things. First, it must study how climate change threatens the people in their area. This study must consider where people live, their race, ethnicity, income level, health, and how racism has affected them in the past and present. Second, it must study how climate change threatens the natural environment in their area, looking at geography and what plants and animals live there. Third, it must study how climate change threatens houses, roads, bridges, and public buildings. Fourth, it must describe what actions they plan to take to address these threats. This might include changing laws about land use, building rules, or building standards. It might also include fixing damaged natural areas and protecting them. When choosing who gets grants, the agency head will give priority to applicants that include environmental justice communities. There is no requirement for applicants to provide matching money. In this law, several terms have specific meanings. Administrator means the head of the Environmental Protection Agency. A climate adaptation plan is a framework for measuring and reducing greenhouse gas pollution and taking steps to adapt to climate change. An eligible entity means a local government, a local government agency, a federally recognized tribal government, or a federally recognized tribe or tribal organization or Alaska Native village that is not a tribal government. An environmental justice community is a community that the Administrator decides has many people of color, low-income people, or tribal or Indigenous people and experiences or risks experiencing more frequent or severe harmful environmental or health effects from climate change. A local government includes counties, cities, towns, townships, school districts, special districts, councils of governments, and regional government groups. A low-income community is a census area where thirty percent or more of people have household incomes at or below the higher of two amounts: eighty percent of the local median income as reported by the Department of Housing and Urban Development, or two hundred percent of the federal poverty line.
